title: andalusia
text: section define individual modelling steps species Pinus halepensis. First, filter records belonging target species. Next, build 10 km buffer around species presences exclude background points close presences. step helps enhance contrast environmental conditions presence background points model needs learn . use buffer identify background points fall inside using sf::st_contains() remove . map shows 10 km buffer around species presences background points remain filtering. finish data preparation combine species presences filtered background single training data frame, remove geometry, select response predictors train binary random forest using predictors, class weights account imbalance presences background records. hyperparameters num.trees = 100 min.node.size = 30 set defaults (500 5, respectively) reduce computation time. point evaluate model via spatial cross-validation, assess variable importance, even check response curves understand ’s working. However, skip steps keep article light content computationally efficient. predict fitted binary model every cell raster andalusia_env. result binary raster values 0 (absence) 1 (presence). presence records target species plotted black. careful values 0 1 map represent pixel values, category labels! convert map numeric pixel value 0 label “0” 1 label “1” remove levels, subtract 1 shift pixel values. Now kind map need next section!
code: species_name <- "Pinus_halepensis" species_presence <- dplyr::filter(   presence,   species == species_name   ) species_presence_buffer <- sf::st_buffer(   x = species_presence,   dist = 10000 #in meters ) |>    sf::st_union() #identify background points contained by the buffer background_inside_buffer <- sf::st_contains(   x = species_presence_buffer,   y = background,   sparse = FALSE ) |>   as.vector()  #select background outside the presence buffer species_background <- background[!background_inside_buffer, ] mapview(   species_presence,    layer.name = "Presence",    col.regions = colors[8]   ) + mapview(   species_presence_buffer,    col.regions = "gray20",    layer.name = "Buffer"   ) +    mapview(     species_background,      col.regions = colors[2],      layer.name = "Background"     ) species_training <- dplyr::bind_rows(   species_presence,   species_background ) |>    sf::st_drop_geometry() |>    dplyr::select(     dplyr::all_of(c("presence", andalusia_predictors))   ) species_model <- ranger::ranger(   dependent.variable.name = "presence",   data = species_training,   case.weights = collinear::case_weights(     x = species_training$presence   ),   num.trees = 100,   min.node.size = 30 ) species_prediction <- terra::predict(   object = andalusia_env,   model = species_model,   na.rm = TRUE )  names(species_prediction) <- species_name plot(   x = species_prediction,   col = c(colors[2], colors[8])   ) points(species_presence) levels(x = species_prediction)[[1]] #>   value label #> 1     1     0 #> 2     2     1 #remove levels levels(x = species_prediction) <- NULL  #shift pixel values species_prediction <- species_prediction - 1 plot(   x = species_prediction,   col = c(colors[2], colors[8])   )

title: communities
text: communities dataset contains plant community records Sierra Nevada mountain range SE Spain. data compiled official vegetation map 1:10,000 scale. designed support multi-class classification, binary presence-absence modelling, ecological niche analysis using climate topographic predictors. main features : Scope: 7,300 point records covering Sierra Nevada mountain range (SE Spain, EPSG:25830). Multi-class binary responses: community column 6 levels ("none" + 5 named plant communities) 5 binary presence-absence columns individual community types. Climate topographic predictors: 9 numeric predictors spanning seasonal temperature extremes, rainfall, terrain attributes. Temporal climate scenario rasters: associated rasters baseline (2010), 2050, 2100 climate scenarios available via download functions.

text: function terra::predict() applies trained random forest model SpatRaster object whose layers share predictor names units used training. output one probability layer per class. transform probabilities class predictions, apply terra::.max() retrieve index layer maximum probability. output prediction raster pixel’s value index layer maximum predicted probability.  facilitate plotting categorical maps, first add category names via terra::categories() categories data frame, second add specific colors per category using terra::coltab(). Let’s take look resulting maps. Let’s transforme pixels plot get better picture changes habitat suitability plant community.  Rather actual community surface given time, curves can interpreted proxy climatic stress: smaller community’s predicted area respect initial area, higher climate stress community may undergo. can also obtain proxy community transitions assessing fraction original area community become suitable another community. transition matrix shows source community (year 2010) first column, resulting community columns, values represent percentage original area source community becoming suitable different community 2050. example, area Alpine pastures transitioning Juniper-broom shrublands can interpreted indicator biological pressure. Environmental change may facilitate upwards migration Juniper-broom species, resulting heightened competition pastures, leading eventual replacement community.
code: probabilities_2010 <- terra::predict(   object = communities_2010,   model  = communities_model,   na.rm  = TRUE )  probabilities_2050 <- terra::predict(   object = communities_2050,   model  = communities_model,   na.rm  = TRUE )  probabilities_2100 <- terra::predict(   object = communities_2100,   model  = communities_model,   na.rm  = TRUE ) plot(   probabilities_2010,    nc = 2,    axes = NULL,   col = env_colors   ) prediction_2010 <- terra::which.max(   x = probabilities_2010 )  prediction_2050 <- terra::which.max(   x = probabilities_2050 )  prediction_2100 <- terra::which.max(   x = probabilities_2100 ) plot(   x = prediction_2010,    col = communities_colors   ) #categories dataframe communities_categories_df <- data.frame(   ID = seq_along(communities_colors),   category = levels(communities$community) )  #colors dataframe communities_colors_df <- data.frame(   value = seq_along(communities_colors),   col = communities_colors )   #assign categories levels(prediction_2010) <- communities_categories_df levels(prediction_2050) <- communities_categories_df levels(prediction_2100) <- communities_categories_df  #assign colors terra::coltab(prediction_2010) <- communities_colors_df terra::coltab(prediction_2050) <- communities_colors_df terra::coltab(prediction_2100) <- communities_colors_df terra::plet(prediction_2010) terra::plet(prediction_2050) terra::plet(prediction_2100) #resolution is 100x100 meters = 1 ha per cell communities_area_df <- dplyr::bind_rows(   terra::freq(prediction_2010) |> dplyr::mutate(year = 2010),   terra::freq(prediction_2050) |> dplyr::mutate(year = 2050),   terra::freq(prediction_2100) |> dplyr::mutate(year = 2100) ) |>   dplyr::rename(community = value) |>   dplyr::mutate(hectares = dplyr::mutate(     transition_percent = round(       x = hectares * 100 / sum(hectares),        digits = 1       )     ) |>   dplyr::select(-hectares) |>    dplyr::ungroup() |>    tidyr::pivot_wider( title: communities
text: habitat suitability projections don’t represent future reality plant communities Sierra Nevada least three reasons: future climate data used result old climate model running one many possible scenarios. model used capture many processes govern vegetation dynamics, including species dispersal limitations, biotic interactions, community resilience adaptation. Random forest predict increased suitability even improving conditions, unlike parametric models GLMs GAMs. communities dataset offers rich testbed multi-class classification ecological niche modelling real Mediterranean mountain system. random forest workflow demonstrates single trained multi-class model can projected across climate scenarios, underscoring importance interpreting projections caution.

title: interaction
text: dataset interaction sf data frame 1000 rows 14 columns. three response variables : class: factor levels "butterfly", "host_plant", "background", identifying observation type row. can interpret background points presence “anything butterfly host plant”. butterfly: integer values 1 0 representing butterfly background records, NA host plant records (simplify subsetting). host_plant: integer values 1 0 indicating host plant presence background records, NA butterfly records. map shows spatial representation class column. dataset includes 10 environmental predictors covering remote sensing, climate, topography 100m resolution.

title: neanderthal
text: dataset sf POINT dataframe (EPSG 4326) 227 rows 28 columns, missing data. response variable presence (see neanderthal_response), integer binary variable indicating Neanderthal presence (1) pseudo-absence (0). locations presence background records mapped . dataset includes 25 palaeoclimatic (last interglacial) topographic predictors. companion raster dataset loaded via neanderthal_extra().

title: neanderthal
text: section use neanderthal model habitat suitability via ensemble univariate GAMs, one per predictor. neanderthal data 49 presences. Fitting model predictors might lead overparameterization overfitting. avoid potential issue, fit univariate GAM models presence environmental predictor. model ensemble follows structure: mgcv::gam(): GAM models replace linear terms used GLMs smoothing splines better represent non-linear relationships presence environment. s(..., k = ceiling(sum(neanderthal$presence)/10): smoothing splines capture non-linear relationships presence predictor. basis dimension k set relative number presences, keeping smooth complexity well within data budget. quasibinomial(link = "logit"): handles overdispersion introduced pseudo-absences fractional case weights, standard binomial family reject. weights = case_weights(...): inverse-frequency weights ensure presences pseudo-absences contribute equally model fit regardless imbalance. select = TRUE: adds shrinkage penalty uninformative smooths can penalized zero, acting automatic term selection. relevant models fitted, ensemble predictions using intrinsic AUC (probability giving higher suitability presence background point) weights.
code: m <- mgcv::gam(   formula = presence ~ s(     bio1,      k = ceiling(sum(neanderthal$presence)/10)     ),   data = sf::st_drop_geometry(neanderthal),   family = stats::quasibinomial(link = "logit"),   weights = collinear::case_weights(     x = neanderthal$presence     ),   select = TRUE ) sample_weights <- collinear::case_weights(x = neanderthal$presence) table(sample_weights) #> sample_weights #> 0.00280898876404494  0.0102040816326531  #>                 178                  49 collinear::score_auc(   o = neanderthal$presence,   p = stats::predict(m, type = "response") ) #> [1] 0.8357028

text: code fits univariate GAM models. Notice models intrinsic AUC 0.75 ignored. Let’s take look performance univariate models. Several models low intrinsic AUC scores, meaning lack ability discriminate properly presences background points. going ignore models AUC 0.75. response curves show predicted probability Neanderthal presence selected univariate models.
code: neanderthal_models <- lapply(   X = neanderthal_selection$presence$selection,   FUN = \(x) {          model_formula <- as.formula(         paste0(           "presence ~ s(",           x,           ", k = ",           ceiling(sum(neanderthal$presence) / 10),           ")"         )       )          model <- mgcv::gam(       formula = model_formula,       data = sf::st_drop_geometry(neanderthal),       family = stats::quasibinomial(link = "logit"),       weights = collinear::case_weights(         x = neanderthal$presence       ),       select = TRUE     )          auc <- collinear::score_auc(       o = neanderthal$presence,       p = stats::predict(         object = model,          type = "response"         )       )          model$auc <- auc          model        } )  names(neanderthal_models) <- neanderthal_selection$presence$selection neanderthal_auc <- lapply(   X = neanderthal_models,   FUN = \(x) x$auc ) |>    unlist()  neanderthal_auc #>          bio17           bio1           bio7           bio8     topo_slope  #>      0.8225178      0.8357028      0.8049759      0.7455859      0.7984407  #>          bio13 topo_diversity   topo_wetness    topo_aspect  #>      0.7860582      0.7043109      0.6113277      0.6315065 #keep models with AUC >= 0.75 neanderthal_auc <- neanderthal_auc[neanderthal_auc >= 0.75]  #remove bad models from the models list neanderthal_models <- neanderthal_models[names(neanderthal_auc)] lapply(   X = names(neanderthal_models),   FUN = \(x) {   x <- plotmo::plotmo(     object = neanderthal_models[[x]],     type = "response",     level = 0.95,     level.shade = "gray80",     main = x,     caption = "",     pt.col = neanderthal$color,     jitter = 0.3,     smooth.col = 0   ) }) |>    invisible()

title: neanderthal
text: build weighted ensemble univariate models first need predict univariate model spatRaster object neanderthal_env. resulting object spatRaster layer represents univariate suitability map.  build ensemble, apply terra::weighted.mean() individual model predictions intrinsic AUCs. map shows high-suitability values warmer colors. finish, can ’s good see: AUC ensemble better AUC univariate models, whole point building kind ensemble modelling.
code: neanderthal_predictions <- lapply(   X = names(neanderthal_models),   FUN = \(x) {     terra::predict(       object = neanderthal_env,       model = neanderthal_models[[x]],       type = "response",       na.rm = TRUE     )   } ) |>    terra::rast()  names(neanderthal_predictions) <- names(neanderthal_models) plot(   x = neanderthal_predictions,   col = neanderthal_colors   ) neanderthal_suitability <- terra::weighted.mean(   x = neanderthal_predictions,   w = neanderthal_auc )  names(neanderthal_suitability) <- "suitability" mapview(   x = neanderthal_suitability,   layer.name = "Suitability",   col.regions = neanderthal_colors,   na.color = "transparent" ) #extract predicted values for all records neanderthal$prediction <- terra::extract(     x = neanderthal_suitability,     y = neanderthal,     ID = FALSE   ) |>      dplyr::pull(       suitability     )  #remove NA data (not supported by score_auc()) neanderthal <- na.omit(neanderthal)  #compute intrinsic AUC collinear::score_auc(   o = neanderthal$presence,   p = neanderthal$prediction ) #> [1] 0.9091353

title: plantae
text: plantae dataset 84 predictors varying levels redundancy. manage multicollinearity plantae use collinear::collinear(). function prioritizes predictors preference_order requested user, remaining ones ranked R-squared univariate random forest models fitted response. predictors evaluated one one selection following ranking: variance inflation factor candidate predictor selected predictors lower 2.5, predictor added selection. Otherwise ignored, next predictor evaluated. function returns list full useful data, focus character vector selected predictor names.
code: plantae_selection <- collinear::collinear(   df = plantae,   responses = "richness_species",   predictors = plantae_predictors,   f = collinear::f_count_rf,   preference_order = c(     "bias_log_records", #logarithm of the GBIF records used to compute richness     "geo_area_km2", #ecoregion area     "climate_velocity_lgm_mean", #climate stability since Last Glacial Maximum     "climate_hypervolume", #relative size of the ecoregion's climate envelope     "temperature_mean", #mean temperature     "precipitation_total", #total rainfall     "human_footprint_mean", #average human footprint in the ecoregion     "human_population_density" #inhabitants per square kilometer   ),   max_vif = 2.5,   quiet = TRUE ) plantae_selection$richness_species$selection #>  [1] "bias_log_records"             "geo_area_km2"                 #>  [3] "climate_velocity_lgm_mean"    "climate_hypervolume"          #>  [5] "temperature_mean"             "precipitation_total"          #>  [7] "human_footprint_mean"         "human_population_density"     #>  [9] "fragmentation_dcore_mn"       "human_population"             #> [11] "landcover_herbs_percent_mean" "fragmentation_shape_mn"       #> [13] "geo_neighbors_count"          "fragmentation_ed"             #> [15] "fragmentation_division"       "geo_perimeter_km"             #> [17] "soil_sand"                    "geo_neighbors_area_km2"       #> [19] "fragmentation_contig_mn"      "geo_elevation_mean"           #> [21] "cloud_cover_range"            "fragmentation_cohesion"       #> [23] "precipitation_seasonality"    "geo_distance_to_ocean"        #> attr(,"validated") #> [1] TRUE

title: plantae
text: spatial autocorrelation (SAC) analysis response selected predictors requires distance matrix pairs ecoregions. Something take mind: distance ecoregions may vary depending geometry used computation. distance neighboring ecoregions zero using polygons resulting plantae_extra(), larger zero using point geometry (ecoregion’s centroid) spatialData::plantae. code uses ecoregion centroids spatialData::plantae compute distance matrix kilometers. distance matrix required assess SAC variable plantae. code analyzes SAC response predictors range neighborhood distances 100 8000 km.  Notice autocorrelation predictors peaks 1000 km, remains positive across entire distance span. analysis suggests data strong spatial structure need account . Let’s first train non-spatial model confirm spatial structure problem. code trains non-spatial random forest model via spatialRF::rf() assessing spatial autocorrelation residuals. model good performance --bag data (RMSE = 1527.11; R-squared = 0.69), random forest models , let’s take look SAC model residuals.  SAC residuals peaks ~1000km remains positive long distances, indicating model missing predictors accounting spatial structure data.
code: plantae_distance <- sf::st_distance(   x = plantae ) |>    units::set_units(value = "km") |>    units::drop_units() |>    floor()  plantae_distance[1:5, 1:5] #>       [,1]  [,2]  [,3]  [,4]  [,5] #> [1,]     0 14610 15776  3358 12011 #> [2,] 14610     0  9068 14138  9480 #> [3,] 15776  9068     0 16377  5361 #> [4,]  3358 14138 16377     0 15220 #> [5,] 12011  9480  5361 15220     0 plantae_distances_km <- c(100, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000)  spatialRF::plot_training_df_moran(   data = plantae,   dependent.variable.name = "richness_species",   predictor.variable.names = plantae_selection$richness_species$selection,   distance.matrix = plantae_distance,   distance.thresholds = plantae_distances_km ) plantae_rf <- spatialRF::rf(   data = sf::st_drop_geometry(plantae),   dependent.variable.name = "richness_species",   predictor.variable.names = plantae_selection$richness_species$selection,   distance.matrix = plantae_distance,   distance.thresholds = c(100, 1000, 2000),   verbose = FALSE ) spatialRF::moran_multithreshold(   x = plantae_rf$residuals$values,   distance.matrix = plantae_distance,   distance.thresholds = plantae_distances_km,   verbose = FALSE )$plot

title: plantae
text: function spatialRF::rf_spatial() takes model , adds spatial predictors (namely Moran’s Eigenvector Maps MEMs) training data. MEMs synthetic predictors derived distance matrix. MEM captures distinct spatial pattern different scale, broad continental gradients finer regional structures. selecting MEMs reduce residual SAC , spatialRF::rf_spatial() captures spatial variation plant richness environmental predictors alone explain. Think biogeographic legacies, dispersal barriers, unknown environmental gradients. Since used distance.thresholds = c(100, 1000, 2000) non-spatial model plantae_rf, function spatialRF::rf_spatial() generate select spatial predictors neighborhood distances 100, 1000, 2000 km. selected distances non-spatial model SAC model residuals centered around values, generating MEMs distances mitigates SAC . model take little train selects smaller subset spatial predictors minimizes spatial autocorrelation residuals . spatial model performance similar non-spatial one (RMSE = 1512.18; R-squared = 0.7). Now, let’s take look SAC model residuals.  Notice values vertical axis: SAC model residuals still significant distance thresholds 1000 2000km, order magnitude smaller non-spatial model (Moran’s ~2.7 non-spatial model ~0.05 spatial one). Otherwise, Moran’s distances 4000 km beyond non-significant now.
code: plantae_rf_spatial <- spatialRF::rf_spatial(   model = plantae_rf,   verbose = FALSE ) spatialRF::moran_multithreshold(   x = plantae_rf_spatial$residuals$values,   distance.matrix = plantae_distance,   distance.thresholds = plantae_distances_km,   verbose = FALSE )$plot

title: quercus
text: Species occurrence counts imbalanced — Q. robur 1,660 records Q. suber 58. Without correction, rare species get absorbed leaves dominated common ones. mitigate issue, use collinear::case_weights() function generate inverse-frequency weights species contributes equally tree splits. decision tree shows environmental variables thresholds best separate oak species. split represents condition (e.g., “bio6 < -2.3”), leaf shows likely species combination conditions.  figure shows different combinations environmental conditions lead dominance different Quercus species terminal node.
code: quercus_rpart <- rpart::rpart(   formula = quercus_selection$species$formulas$classification,   data = sf::st_drop_geometry(quercus),   method = "class",   weights = collinear::case_weights(x = quercus$species) ) rpart.plot::rpart.plot(   quercus_rpart,   type = 0,   extra = 8,   fallen.leaves = TRUE,   roundint = FALSE,   cex = 0.7,   box.palette = as.list(quercus_colors),   main = "Decision Tree: European Oak Species Classification" )

title: quercus
text: prediction model quercus_rpart quercus_env generates spatRaster layer represents presence probability single species. can extract class highest probability cell. facilitate identification class labels, need add category names terra::categories() colors per category using terra::coltab(). Now can replot map correct class labels. Notice binary splits rpart generate box-like artifacts map, represent specific map splits. example, horizontal limit Quercus cerris Quercus robur corresponds split sun_rad_minimum >= 773. Recursive partition trees fast train, really good offering simple intuitive explanation species , best possible method generate accurate spatial predictions.
code: quercus_probability <- predict(   object = quercus_env,   model = quercus_rpart,   type = "prob",   na.rm = TRUE )  plot(   x = quercus_probability,   col = env_colors   ) quercus_class <- terra::which.max(   x = quercus_probability ) plot(   x = quercus_class,    col = quercus_colors   ) quercus_categories_df <- data.frame(   ID = seq_along(quercus_colors),   category = names(quercus_colors) )  communities_colors_df <- data.frame(   value = seq_along(quercus_colors),   col = quercus_colors )  levels(quercus_class) <- quercus_categories_df terra::coltab(quercus_class) <- communities_colors_df terra::plet(quercus_class)

title: vi

title: vi
text: code computes distance matrix locations vi need assess spatial auto-correlation (SAC) model residuals. also need geographic coordinates cases, encoded well-known text geometry column.
code: vi_distance_matrix_meters <- vi |>    sf::st_distance() |>    unclass() #removes units (meters)  vi_distance_matrix_km <- vi_distance_matrix_meters / 1000 rm(vi_distance_matrix_meters) vi_coordinates <- vi |>    sf::st_coordinates() |>    as.data.frame() |>    dplyr::rename(     latitude = Y,     longitude = X   )  vi <- dplyr::bind_cols(   vi,   vi_coordinates )

title: vi
text: Let’s use formula fit regular GAM model environmental predictors . model looks impressive, let’s take look SAC model residuals.  SAC model’s residuals quite high! indicates model achieving good fit partly exploiting spatial proximity rather genuine environmental signal. SAC likely compressing standard errors pushing p-values , making smooth terms look significant truly . Let’s take look approach might help mitigate problem.
code: vi_model_non_spatial <- mgcv::gam(   formula = vi_formula,   data = sf::st_drop_geometry(vi),   select = TRUE )  summary(vi_model_non_spatial) #>  #> Family: gaussian  #> Link function: identity  #>  #> Formula: #> vi_numeric ~ s(rainfall_mean) + s(soil_temperature_max) + s(swi_max) +  #>     s(temperature_seasonality) + s(humidity_range) + s(topo_diversity) +  #>     s(topo_elevation) + s(cloud_cover_range) + s(soil_clay) +  #>     s(soil_silt) #>  #> Parametric coefficients: #>              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)     #> (Intercept) 0.4502148  0.0007175   627.5   <2e-16 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Approximate significance of smooth terms: #>                                  edf Ref.df       F  p-value     #> s(rainfall_mean)           7.290e+00      9 132.908  < 2e-16 *** #> s(soil_temperature_max)    7.897e+00      9 415.469  < 2e-16 *** #> s(swi_max)                 8.545e+00      9  54.352  < 2e-16 *** #> s(temperature_seasonality) 8.528e+00      9 118.859  < 2e-16 *** #> s(humidity_range)          5.362e+00      9  23.442  < 2e-16 *** #> s(topo_diversity)          5.303e+00      9   3.634 2.45e-06 *** #> s(topo_elevation)          7.796e+00      9 118.771  < 2e-16 *** #> s(cloud_cover_range)       7.877e+00      9  10.239  < 2e-16 *** #> s(soil_clay)               2.503e-05      9   0.000    0.878     #> s(soil_silt)               6.242e+00      9   7.781  < 2e-16 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> R-sq.(adj) =   0.86   Deviance explained = 86.1% #> GCV = 0.0048036  Scale est. = 0.0047694  n = 9265 vi_model_non_spatial_sac <- spatialRF::moran_multithreshold(   x = stats::residuals(vi_model_non_spatial),   distance.matrix = vi_distance_matrix_km,   distance.thresholds = c(100, 500, 1000, 2500, 5000) )

title: vi
text: idea provide GAM Model predictor representing spatial proximity records. predictor absorb part overall explained variance, resulting honest effect estimates environmental predictors. approach add term s(latitude, longitude, bs = "sos") original model formula. smooth---sphere interaction original latitudes longitudes degrees. term needs also argument k (number knots) value high enough give GAM flexibility required match autocorrelation structure data. case vi, width 40000km, height 12000km (check sf::st_bbox(vi)), SAC still significant 1000km, can think imaginary grid cell size 1000x1000km, 40 * 12 cells total 480. compose new formula, first need convert original formula character (paste(deparse(...))), add new term. Let’s take look new model! Notice individual smooth terms explain less variance non-spatial model. s(latitude, longitude) term absorbing part geographic structure environmental predictors capturing , expected. goal maximize R², get effect estimates reflect genuine environmental signal rather spatial proximity.  SAC residuals much lower now, indicating spatial term model great job! Now, curious spatial term looks like, map shows contribution predicted NDVI environmental predictors factored . Positive values (teal) mark areas vegetation productive local conditions alone predict, negative values (brown) mark opposite. likely causes things predictors don’t measure, land use history, soil legacy effects, plant adaptations specific climates.
code: vi_formula_spatial <- formula(   paste(     paste(deparse(vi_formula), collapse  = " "),     "+ s(latitude, longitude, bs = 'sos', k = 480)"   ) )  vi_formula_spatial #> vi_numeric ~ s(rainfall_mean) + s(soil_temperature_max) + s(swi_max) +  #>     s(temperature_seasonality) + s(humidity_range) + s(topo_diversity) +  #>     s(topo_elevation) + s(cloud_cover_range) + s(soil_clay) +  #>     s(soil_silt) + s(latitude, longitude, bs = "sos", k = 480) vi_model_spatial <- mgcv::gam(   formula = vi_formula_spatial,    data = sf::st_drop_geometry(vi),   select = TRUE   )  summary(vi_model_spatial) #>  #> Family: gaussian  #> Link function: identity  #>  #> Formula: #> vi_numeric ~ s(rainfall_mean) + s(soil_temperature_max) + s(swi_max) +  #>     s(temperature_seasonality) + s(humidity_range) + s(topo_diversity) +  #>     s(topo_elevation) + s(cloud_cover_range) + s(soil_clay) +  #>     s(soil_silt) + s(latitude, longitude, bs = "sos", k = 480) #>  #> Parametric coefficients: #>              Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)     #> (Intercept) 0.4502148  0.0005666   794.5   <2e-16 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> Approximate significance of smooth terms: #>                                  edf Ref.df       F  p-value     #> s(rainfall_mean)           7.066e+00      9  28.597  < 2e-16 *** #> s(soil_temperature_max)    7.924e+00      9 407.582  < 2e-16 *** #> s(swi_max)                 5.594e+00      9  32.252  < 2e-16 *** #> s(temperature_seasonality) 5.380e+00      9   3.461 1.76e-06 *** #> s(humidity_range)          4.161e+00      9   4.964  < 2e-16 *** #> s(topo_diversity)          4.952e+00      9   7.032  < 2e-16 *** #> s(topo_elevation)          7.640e+00      9 159.528  < 2e-16 *** #> s(cloud_cover_range)       2.637e-05      9   0.000    0.444     #> s(soil_clay)               3.956e-05      9   0.000    0.898     #> s(soil_silt)               4.426e+00      9   3.673 2.19e-07 *** #> s(latitude,longitude)      4.127e+02    479  12.467  < 2e-16 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #>  #> R-sq.(adj) =  0.913   Deviance explained = 91.7% #> GCV = 0.0031304  Scale est. = 0.0029747  n = 9265 vi_model_spatial_sac <- spatialRF::moran_multithreshold(   x = stats::residuals(vi_model_spatial),   distance.matrix = vi_distance_matrix_km,   distance.thresholds = c(100, 500, 1000, 2500, 5000) ) vi$spatial_effect_sos <- mgcv::predict.gam(   object = vi_model_spatial,   type = "terms",   terms = "s(latitude,longitude)" )[, 1]  mapview::mapview(   vi,   zcol = "spatial_effect_sos",   layer.name = "Spatial component",   col.regions = grDevices::hcl.colors(     n = 10,     palette = "Green-Brown",     rev = TRUE   ) )

title: vi
text: code predicts models vi plot observed vs predicted NDVI. models pretty good job matching predictions observations, spatial model shows narrower band tightly grouped around diagonal.
code: vi_long <- vi |>   dplyr::mutate(     non_spatial = stats::fitted(vi_model_non_spatial),     spatial = stats::fitted(vi_model_spatial)   ) |>   tidyr::pivot_longer(     cols = c("non_spatial", "spatial"),     names_to = "model",     values_to = "prediction"   )  ggplot2::ggplot(vi_long) +   ggplot2::aes(     x = vi_numeric,     y = prediction,     color = vi_numeric   ) +   ggplot2::facet_wrap(     facets = ~model,     ncol = 2   ) +   ggplot2::geom_point(     alpha = 0.5,     size = 0.5   ) +   ggplot2::scale_color_continuous(     palette = vi_colors   ) +   ggplot2::geom_abline(     slope = 1,     intercept = 0,     linetype = "dashed"   ) +   ggplot2::labs(     title = "Observations vs. Predictions",     x = "Observed NDVI",     y = "Predicted NDVI"   ) +   ggplot2::coord_equal() +   ggplot2::theme_bw() +    ggplot2::theme(legend.position = "none")

what: Plant diversity metrics for the World's Ecoregions — plantae
title: Plant diversity metrics for the World's Ecoregions — plantae
text: Plant diversity metrics (richness, rarity, beta diversity) obtained GBIF Plantae records World's Ecoregions. Includes metrics plants, trees, grasses species, genus, family taxonomic levels. Ecoregion boundaries derived Ecoregions 2017. Original polygon geometries converted point centroids reduce file size preserving spatial context. Use plantae_extra() download full version original polygon geometries. datasets plantae_west plantae_east subsets plantae focused overall plant richness western easter hemispheres, respectively. GBIF download comprised 244,830,168 records 4,741 datasets, filtered records coordinates, geospatial issues, occurrence status "present". Tree species identified cross-referencing GBIF records BGCI Global Tree Search database (BGCI 2020). Grasses defined members family Poaceae. Rarity-weighted richness computed taxon inverse number spatial presence records GBIF, scores summed per ecoregion, mean rarity mean inverse presence record counts per taxon ecoregion. Beta diversity computed ecoregion immediate neighboring ecoregions via Sorensen dissimilarity (Bsor = 1 - 2a/(2a+b+c))  Simpson dissimilarity (Bsim = min(b,c)/(min(b,c)+)), following Koleff et al. (2003). Fragmentation metrics computed R package landscapemetrics (Hesselbarth et al. 2019) 5 km resolution Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area projection. Climate hypervolume computed using hypervolume::hypervolume_svm() climate predictors. Aridity computed 1 minus aridity index Trabucco Zomer (2019), maximum aridity coded 1. Environmental predictors extracted mean pixel values per ecoregion rasters 1 km resolution.

Penman-Monteith). evapotranspiration_range: Annual potential evapotranspiration range (kg m-2 month-1; Penman-Monteith). cloud_cover_mean: Mean annual total cloud cover (%). cloud_cover_max: Maximum monthly total cloud cover (%). cloud_cover_min: Minimum monthly total cloud cover (%). cloud_cover_range: Annual total cloud cover range (%). aridity_index: Mean aridity index (unitless ratio; higher values indicate wetter conditions). humidity_mean: Mean annual near-surface relative humidity (%). humidity_max: Maximum monthly near-surface relative humidity (%). humidity_min: Minimum monthly near-surface relative humidity (%). humidity_range: Annual near-surface relative humidity range (%). biogeo_ecoregion: Ecoregion name. biogeo_biome: Biome name. biogeo_realm: Ecological realm name. country_name: Country name. continent: Continent name. region: UN region name. subregion: UN sub-region name. title: spatialData 1.0.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-04-21 First release spatialData CRAN, following datasets: neanderthal: Neanderthal presence/pseudo-absence records 25 palaeoclimatic topographic predictors (neanderthal_response, neanderthal_predictors). vi: Global NDVI records 58 environmental predictors (vi_responses, vi_predictors, vi_extra()). quercus: Eight European oak species distribution 31 environmental predictors (quercus_response, quercus_predictors, quercus_extra()). plantae: Plant diversity metrics 662 global ecoregions 84 environmental predictors (plantae_responses, plantae_predictors, plantae_extra(), plantae_west(), plantae_east()). trees: Tree species richness Mesoamerican trees 50 environmental predictors (trees_response, trees_predictors, trees_extra()). communities: Plant communities Sierra Nevada (SE Spain) 9 climate topographic predictors (communities_responses, communities_predictors, communities_extra_2010(), communities_extra_2050(), communities_extra_2100()). interaction: Butterfly-host plant co-occurrence Sierra Nevada 10 environmental predictors (interaction_responses, interaction_predictors, interaction_extra()). andalusia: Presence records 90 threatened plant species Andalusia 20 environmental predictors (andalusia_responses, andalusia_predictors, andalusia_extra()). linaria: Linaria nigricans presence records Eastern Andalusia 20 environmental predictors (linaria_responses, linaria_predictors, linaria_extra()).
